# **Why you shouldn’t use Dribbble and Behance?**

[![Patryk Nawrocki](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*1bcBAwNtRQM4Ni2NbzRUKw.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@patryk.nawrocki?source=post_page---byline--ec6782b2300---------------------------------------)

[Patryk Nawrocki](https://medium.com/@patryk.nawrocki?source=post_page---byline--ec6782b2300---------------------------------------)

Follow

4 min read

·

Jul 10, 2022

552

13

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3Dec6782b2300&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fuxplanet.org%2Fwhy-you-shouldnt-use-dribbble-and-behance-ec6782b2300&source=---header_actions--ec6782b2300---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*bctUMY9zXVf6ADrud7JuGg.png)

Have you ever wondered what’s wrong with all these fancy designs that you see on Dribble or Behance?

It can really hit **your self-esteem** and bring **wrong assumptions** to a new designer and an experienced one. Most of the projects on these websites are bending the reality of UX and focusing so much to make something look like a piece of art. As long as you **treat this as UI inspiration**, this is not that bad. Side effects appear if you look at these projects like it’s thoughtful UX work.

## **A side effect of treating product design as an art**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*2xdw2VZ9AtgsngkhEEDC2A.png)

Have you ever tried to design a certain solution after spending time on dribbble? It’s so easy to blame yourself because our design is not looking like projects that you’ve seen. But do you know how these screens are made? The designer picks a few features or patterns that… fit well with the mockup. Most of that what you see is made like this. **There is no thinking about who would use the product.** The designer just uses patterns that can look good and ta-dah!

## Get Patryk Nawrocki’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

But how does it affect your work? Let’s say you’ve got a job to do — design a homepage for the client’s product. **Ask yourself** if your process doesn’t look like this:

- 👉🏻 1\. Open a platform like dribbble
- 👉🏻 2\. Pick the projects that your eyes really like.
- 👉🏻 3\. Decide what you would like to use in your design from it.
- 👉🏻 4\. Run Figma and start design.
- 😱 5\. But wait… what the hell should be on this website, why and what’s the architecture’s information? What is the CTA (call to action) that we want to place?

**And you realize the UX of your design is a step that you completely skip.** If you focus on the UI at first and later you’re trying to fit the UX into thisthen I would recommend taking a step back and reversing your process. It’s so easy to make a product that looks good **but no one will use this**. The design job should start in your mind where you ask questions like what and why.

## **Reality check — your design will not be implemented**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*3Gbf1PXKEaCNMBaqa7ZDzA.png)

If you go to school at 13 years old that try to fit into the environment then you find out smoking cigarettes, skipping lessons, and bullying others are actions that get you to respect from others. You don’t care if it is good or not — **it works** and people like you. After some time you understand how stupid and unnecessary it was.

**The same thing happens to you** if you’re a designer.Focusing on that how things looks instead of how things should work and why. You feel proud about your job because it’s pretty until you ask yourselfwhy and for what you’ve designed it. Of course, if you want to be specifically only a UI designer then the knowledge about UX is not that much required from you _(but unfortunately job market would be smaller)._

There is a skill that you need to knowas both a UI and UX designer. **Knowledge about what is easy to implement** vswhat is hard and requires spending a lot of money and time. This is something that you will do in your whole career — designing solutions that are easy to implement and satisfy you.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*RzXBOD4zdgO1M8zdXE0lPw.png)

But wait, how does this relate to the Dribbble or Behance? Unfortunately, I have to disappoint you but most of the projects that you see **can’t be done in real life.** Flying 3D objects that follow your mouse are cool but the design is a business and someone needs to hire developers to code it. So if the developer can complete **important features** in a month or spend that time making cool animations then I guess it’s clear what’s a rational choice.

The same thing is with designing certain components — you can make an abstract, unique pattern that will cost 6 times more to code or just focus on patterns that look great and **are easy to make.** Real-life would verify your design a lot of time and you need to find a proper balance to make something that satisfies you and it’s possible to bring this to the working product.

# I Spent 3 Hours on Dribbble. Claude Solved My Design Problem in 10 Minutes.

## AI isn’t replacing UX designers. But if you’re still designing the old way, you’re already behind.

[![Dolphia](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*ey-6cJEKXxjcSu1Jhvbfgg.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@dolphia.n.arnstein?source=post_page---byline--6d08f7d5de3e---------------------------------------)

[Dolphia](https://medium.com/@dolphia.n.arnstein?source=post_page---byline--6d08f7d5de3e---------------------------------------)

I was three hours into a Dribbble spiral when I realized I was procrastinating.

You know the routine: designing a web app, need inspiration, so you fall into the infinite scroll. Purple gradients. Glassmorphism. Animated micro-interactions that would take days to build and add zero user value.

Three hours. Dozens of tabs. Zero useful ideas.

Out of desperation, I opened Claude.

“I’m designing a dashboard for a project management tool. Users are overwhelmed by information. I need to prioritize what matters most without hiding critical data. Give me three conceptual approaches.”

Ten minutes later, I had three completely different design directions. Each tailored to my specific problem. Each considering my constraints. Each better than anything I’d found scrolling through portfolios designed for completely different contexts.

This wasn’t just faster. It was _better_.

## The Shift

Here’s what nobody tells you about AI in UX: it’s not about the output. It’s about the conversation.

Where Dribbble gives me aesthetics, Claude gives me strategy. Where design galleries show trends, Claude helps me solve actual problems.

I’ll tell it: ‘I’m designing the health insurance selection flow for an HR portal. Users are overwhelmed by plan options with confusing terminology. They need to compare coverage but don’t understand deductibles vs. premiums. How do I simplify decision-making without oversimplifying critical information?’

It comes back with approaches I hadn’t considered. Patterns that account for stress, time pressure, and error prevention. Ideas rooted in actual constraints, not just portfolio aesthetics.

**This is what AI does well: brainstorming that understands context.**

## What Most Designers Get Wrong

Here’s what I see happening that will hurt careers:

**Using AI as a replacement instead of a tool.** Some designers treat it like a magic button. Prompt in, design out, ship it. That’s not designing. That’s outsourcing your brain.

**Blindly accepting outputs.** AI doesn’t do user research. It doesn’t watch people struggle in usability tests. I’ve seen designers ship AI-generated interfaces that look slick but completely miss the user’s context.

**Not understanding limitations.** AI suggests patterns. Only users tell you if they work. I still do research, testing, and iteration. AI just helps me get to better starting points, faster.

> **The rule: AI helps you think faster. It doesn’t think for you.**

## How I Actually Use It

I use Figma AI for quick iterations. Uizard for rapid prototyping. But Claude is where I think.

I start with problems, not tools. “Here’s my user’s problem, here are constraints, here’s what I’ve tried. What am I missing?”

Claude helps me brainstorm 10 directions in the time it used to take to sketch three. But I decide which is right. I know the users, the business constraints, the technical limitations.

And I validate everything with humans.

## The Truth

AI isn’t replacing UX designers. But it’s raising the bar for what “good enough” looks like.

Five years ago, creating a clean interface was enough. Now, anyone with AI can do that. The differentiator isn’t aesthetics anymore. It’s judgment. Strategy. Deep understanding of users.

The designers who’ll thrive use AI to think faster and deeper — not to avoid thinking at all.

Those who struggle are still spending three hours on Dribbble looking for the perfect gradient.

I’m no longer scrolling for hours, hoping inspiration strikes. I’m having a conversation with an AI that understands my constraints and helps me explore solutions I wouldn’t have found alone.

Then I do the human work: researching, testing, iterating, and making judgment calls AI can’t.

**That’s the future. And if you’re not experimenting with this yet, you’re already behind.**

_I’m a UX designer who creates websites and web apps. I use AI tools like Claude, Figma AI, and Uizard — not to replace my thinking, but to amplify it._

# Why a designer needs to be on Dribbble

## Half of your competition is already there.

[![Kalpesh Prithyani](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*YPjPG0L4uPpk25hZmXQCKA.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@kalpeshprithyani?source=post_page---byline--63e1d8402e2---------------------------------------)

[Kalpesh Prithyani](https://medium.com/@kalpeshprithyani?source=post_page---byline--63e1d8402e2---------------------------------------)

Follow

4 min read

·

Aug 25, 2019

149

1

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D63e1d8402e2&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fuxdesign.cc%2Fwhy-a-designer-needs-to-be-on-dribbble-63e1d8402e2&source=---header_actions--63e1d8402e2---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*3jjcu9Dty73FLoS0ulOjYw.jpeg)

Also, I have a Dribbble invite

Are you even in the design industry if you are not on Dribbble?

> “But I am on Behance.”
>
> “But I work as a UX designer.”
>
> “But why do I even need to share my work”
>
> “But I am happy with my company, why would I need a portfolio”

Tch. Tch. Tch…! 🤫

No exceptions.

It is 2019. You have to market yourself. No one is going to come to you and say “can you show me your work? I want you in my next big project”. And with the software development industry reaching a saturation level, there are a lot of emerging designers. A successful Dribbble profile is one way to set yourself apart.

## How can Dribbble help me?

1. Instagram and Behance are open doors for everyone, anyone can post an illustration. But Dribbble is a limited community, only a community member can invite you to the court. So just by being a player at Dribbble means you are already above those ordinary designers who just know the tools of Photoshop and Illustrator.
2. You get instant honest feedback from the design community. No clients. No funny people with business hats are going to judge the viability of your project.
3. The limitation on the pixel size actually makes you more creative. It makes you think what good can you put in that 4:3 frame that will make people click.
4. Seeing the number of views and likes on your project gives you confidence that you are heading in the right direction. Plus the amount of motivation you get seeing lit work from other designers really gets you going.
5. Dribbble community is growing rapidly. Are you seeing this insane graph? The community size just doubled in 2018 and it is supposed to keep increasing in 2019 as well. With a lot of new audiences, it is the best time to make your presence felt in the design world.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*3d18EljzDqBTpnJXRgfSjA.jpeg)

Source: [Dribbble](https://dribbble.com/year/2018/)

Half of your competition is already there, making an audience for themselves.

## Get Kalpesh Prithyani’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

A portfolio says so much about you. It says how diverse your work is. It says that there are ideas constantly striking your head and that you are doing something about them.

Surely you can keep your work to yourself and show it directly in some interview. But then, are you really a designer if don’t want to showcase your work? Showcasing your work means that you are open to feedback, that you are confident about your design, that you have one final output of the N number of hours you gave in that project. If you don’t show the world what you are cable of, nobody is going to notice you.

Even if you are a UX designer, you need to be on Dribbble. Bombard your account with wireframes. People are hungry for good content. They will even love those boxes and lines when put out the right way. And if you are a UX designer, you know there is a lot more to showcase.

Also, Dribbble’s interface is a charm! 😍

## How Dribbble works?

There are two sides to Dribbble. Prospects and Players.

**Prospects**: They can upload their shots but Dribbble doesn’t promote them in the news feed. The shots are only visible on the profile page and on the draft page (which people rarely visit). Prospects can’t comment on posts.

**Players**: They are the actual community members. Work posted by Players appears on the feed page of other designers, which means more reach and feedback.

In order to be drafted as a Player, Prospects need a Dribbble invite which can be passed on to you by any of the Players (or you can subscribe to Dribbble’s pro plan). I know it’s a curse as some of the remarkable talents might be left undrafted. But it’s actually a blessing in disguise, it helps filter out the not-so-creative work, so you can see the rich interesting content.

So, here is the news, I have **one Dribbble invite**. In case you are interested, send me your best shot along with your Dribbble profile link at _kalpeshprithyani@gmail.com by_ Aug 27.

If you have any questions, feel free to comment ✌

PS. If you ever get an invitation, try to make the best out of your debut shot as it gets promoted easily through Dribbble’s algorithm. Shoot a 3 pointer and the game will change for you.

**Thanks for the 👏 if you enjoyed the article. You can also follow me on** [**Dribbble**](https://dribbble.com/kalpeshPrithyani) **and** [**Instagram**](https://www.instagram.com/kalpesh.prithyani/) **.**
